name: Quarterback
responsibilities: Receives snap from center and initiates every offensive play
Reads the defense pre-snap to identify coverages and blitzes
Hands off to the running back, throws to a receiver, or scrambles
Calls audibles at the line to change the play based on defensive alignment
notes: The most important position on the field. A QB who can identify pre-snap looks — like a safety cheating toward the box — gives the offense a huge advantage.

name: Running Back
responsibilities: Carries the ball on run plays
Blocks for the QB on passing plays
Runs routes as a checkdown option out of the backfield
Picks up blitzing defenders in pass protection
notes: In the I-Formation, the RB lines up behind the fullback and QB. In Shotgun, they line up beside the QB. Their alignment signals run vs. pass tendencies.

name: Fullback
responsibilities: Lead blocker on power run plays — clears a path for the RB
Short-yardage runner on goal line and 4th-and-1 situations
Occasionally runs routes as a receiving option
notes: Largely phased out in modern football but critical in power-run formations like the I-Formation and Goal Line. Defenses must account for the FB or give up easy short-yardage gains.

name: Wide Receiver
responsibilities: Runs precise routes to create separation from defensive backs
Catches passes from the QB
Blocks on run plays when the ball goes to their side
notes: WR alignments tell you a lot. A 'trips' set (three WRs to one side) forces the defense to choose: match up or leave one side vulnerable.

name: Tight End
responsibilities: Blocks defensive ends and linebackers on run plays
Runs routes as a receiving option in the passing game
Can be used as an extra blocker (inline TE) or split wide like a WR
notes: The TE is a matchup problem: too big for cornerbacks, too fast for linebackers. Their alignment — inline vs. flexed out — telegraphs whether a play is run-heavy or pass-heavy.

name: Left Tackle
responsibilities: Protects the QB's blind side (for a right-handed QB)
Opens running lanes on plays to the left
Often assigned to block the opposing team's best pass rusher
notes: The most valuable offensive lineman. A weak left tackle will be exposed by speed rushers off the edge — a key exploitation in blitz-heavy schemes.

name: Left Guard
responsibilities: Blocks the interior defensive linemen and inside linebackers
Pulls on run plays to lead block around the edge
notes: Guards and centers form the interior of the offensive line — if they lose to nose tackles or interior DTs, run plays get stuffed at the line of scrimmage.

name: Center
responsibilities: Snaps the ball to the QB to begin every play
Identifies defensive fronts and communicates blocking assignments to the line
Blocks the nose tackle on run plays
notes: The center is the offensive line's quarterback. They make the blocking calls that allow the whole line to work as a unit.

name: Right Guard
responsibilities: Blocks interior defensive linemen
Pulls on run plays to lead block
notes: See Left Guard. The RG mirrors the LG across the center.

name: Right Tackle
responsibilities: Protects the QB's front side
Opens running lanes to the right
Blocks edge rushers off the right side
notes: Generally faces the defense's second-best pass rusher. Run plays to the right depend heavily on the RT winning at the point of attack.

name: Defensive End
responsibilities: Rushes the passer off the edge
Sets the edge on run plays — prevents the runner from getting outside
Maintains gap assignment on run defense
notes: In a 4-3, the two DEs are primary pass rushers. In a 3-4, the ends are heavier run-stuffers; pass rushing falls to the outside linebackers.

name: Defensive Tackle
responsibilities: Occupies offensive linemen to free up linebackers
Penetrates gaps to stop runs in the backfield (tackles for loss)
Interior pass rush — collapse the pocket up the middle
notes: A dominant DT (like a '3-technique' who aligns in the gap outside the guard) can wreck run-heavy offenses by collapsing gaps before blockers can reach linebackers.

name: Nose Tackle
responsibilities: Lines up over the center in 3-4 and goal-line fronts
Occupies two blockers to free linebackers
Plugs the A-gaps (gaps on either side of the center)
notes: The NT is a 'space-eater.' They don't make many flashy plays — their job is to draw double-teams so linebackers can flow freely to the ball.

name: Middle Linebacker
responsibilities: Reads the offensive backfield to diagnose run or pass pre-snap
Fills run gaps when the DL creates openings
Covers the middle of the field on pass plays (zones) or follows the RB (man)
notes: The 'quarterback of the defense.' The MLB makes defensive adjustments at the line of scrimmage in the same way the QB calls audibles.

name: Outside Linebacker
responsibilities: Sets the edge against outside runs
In 3-4 defenses: rushes the passer off the edge
Covers tight ends or running backs in pass coverage
notes: In a 3-4, the OLBs are often the primary pass rushers (effectively acting as edge rushers). In a 4-3, they play more as run-stoppers and coverage LBs.

name: Cornerback
responsibilities: Covers wide receivers in man-to-man or zone coverage
Jams WRs at the line of scrimmage to disrupt route timing
Sets the edge as the last line of defense on outside runs
notes: CBs must have short memories — getting beaten for a deep ball is part of the job. Elite corners eliminate the opponent's best WR from the game.

name: Strong Safety
responsibilities: Lines up near the line of scrimmage on the tight end side
Supports run defense — acts as an extra linebacker near the box
Covers tight ends or short crossing routes in the middle
notes: The SS 'in the box' is a tell that the defense is loading up to stop the run. Offenses that see this can audible to a quick pass to exploit single coverage outside.

name: Free Safety
responsibilities: Lines up deep in the middle of the field
Reads the QB's eyes and reacts to deep routes
Provides over-the-top help to cornerbacks
Last line of defense against big plays
notes: In Cover 2, both safeties split the deep field. In Cover 1 (man free), the FS roams free while everyone else plays man. The FS alignment tells an experienced QB a lot about coverage.

name: Nickelback
responsibilities: Fifth defensive back, replaces a linebacker in Nickel packages
Covers slot receivers, tight ends, and running backs in space
Occasional blitzer from the slot (nickel blitz)
notes: The 'nickel' package exists because a standard LB struggles to cover fast slot WRs in space. Offenses that put three or more WRs on the field force defenses to sub in the nickelback.
